abilities: Fire Breathing: Devastating flames from the lion's maw
Triple Threat: Three heads allow attacks from multiple directions
Goat Agility: Mountain goat body provides sure-footedness
Serpent Tail: Venomous strikes from behind
Terrifying Presence: The unnatural combination inspires fear
Flight (in some interpretations, due to its monstrous nature)
name: Chimera

christian.overview: The Chimera, with its lion, goat, and serpent components breathing fire, prefigures later Christian iconography of composite beasts representing sin, heresy, or demonic powers. Its defeat by Bellerophon was read as a type of Christ's victory over evil.

biblicalParallels.description: The apocalyptic horses with lion heads and serpent tails that breathe fire echo the Chimera's composite, fire-breathing nature.
Daniel's vision of composite beasts rising from chaos anticipates the Chimera tradition of unnatural combinations representing worldly empires opposed to God.
The peaceable kingdom where predators and prey coexist contrasts with the Chimera's violent fusion, suggesting that sin creates unnatural combinations while redemption restores proper order.

patristicInterpretation.summary: Church Fathers saw the Chimera as representing the disordered passions - multiple drives warring within a single being - overcome only by virtue (Bellerophon on Pegasus representing reason elevated by grace).

sources.author: Fulgentius
sources.interpretation: Interpreted the Chimera allegorically: the lion head represents fierce anger (ira), the goat body represents lustful passion (libido), and the serpent tail represents the sting of consequences (dolus).

sources.author: Isidore of Seville
sources.interpretation: The Chimera represents the stages of human life consumed by passion: youth's fierceness (lion), middle age's sensuality (goat), and old age's cunning (serpent).

medievalSymbolism.bestiaryMeaning: Medieval bestiaries used the Chimera as an example of impossibility and deception - its existence was sometimes denied, seen as a figure for lies that seem real.
medievalSymbolism.moralLesson: The Chimera teaches that sin creates monsters within us - unnatural fusions of conflicting desires that can only be slain by the hero of virtue mounted on the divine Pegasus of grace.
medievalSymbolism.artisticRepresentation: Gothic cathedral sculptures often included chimeric creatures in portrayals of hell and damnation, representing the monstrous transformations wrought by sin.

christian.typologicalReading: Bellerophon's aerial defeat of the Chimera (attacking from above, out of reach of the fire) prefigures Christ's victory from the Cross - striking at sin from a position (the Cross/throne) that sin cannot reach.
scholarlyReferences: Chance, Jane. Medieval Mythography: From Roman North Africa to the School of Chartres. Gainesville: University Press of Florida, 1994.
Rahner, Hugo. Greek Myths and Christian Mystery. New York: Harper & Row, 1963.

keyMyths.title: Bellerophon and the Chimera
keyMyths.description: King Iobates of Lycia ordered Bellerophon to slay the Chimera, hoping he would perish in the attempt. Aided by Pegasus and Athena's advice, Bellerophon ultimately defeated the beast by melting lead down its throat.

keyMyths.title: The Chimera's Lineage
keyMyths.description: The Chimera is described as the offspring of Typhon and Echidna, monstrous beings that spawned many of the most fearsome creatures in Greek mythology. This parentage highlights the Chimera's inherently chaotic and destructive nature.

keyMyths.title: Omens of the Chimera
keyMyths.description: While not explicitly detailed, it is inferred that the Chimera's presence was considered a bad omen, a sign of impending disaster or divine wrath. Its defeat by Bellerophon was a victory for order over chaos.
keyMyths.source: Inferences based on the creature's monstrous nature and the heroes who fought it

keyMyths.title: The Lycian Landscape
keyMyths.description: The Chimera is said to have terrorized the region of Lycia, specifically around the Cragus mountains. Its fiery breath scorched the land, making it a dangerous and desolate place.

keyMyths.title: Representations in Art
keyMyths.description: The Chimera appears in various forms of ancient Greek art, including pottery and sculpture. These depictions often emphasize its composite nature, showcasing the lion's head, goat's body, and serpent's tail.
keyMyths.source: Archaeological evidence and art historical analyses

sources.name: Homer, *Iliad*
sources.description: An epic poem providing an early account of Bellerophon's battle with the Chimera.
sources.title: Homer, *Iliad*

sources.name: Hesiod, *Theogony*
sources.description: A genealogical account of the gods, including the Chimera's monstrous parentage.
sources.title: Hesiod, *Theogony*

sources.name: Hyginus, *Fabulae*
sources.description: A collection of mythological summaries, offering details about the Chimera's location and defeat.
sources.title: Hyginus, *Fabulae*

sources.name: Pierre Grimal, *The Dictionary of Classical Mythology*
sources.description: A comprehensive modern encyclopedia of Greek and Roman mythology.
sources.title: Pierre Grimal, *The Dictionary of Classical Mythology*

sources.name: Carlos Parada, *Greek Mythology Link*
sources.description: A detailed online resource compiling information from various ancient sources.
sources.title: Carlos Parada, *Greek Mythology Link*
